text:
Where deaths occur along borders due to unsafe or poor health conditions caused by the irregularisation of their journey, border deaths function in a particular way in the public sphere: they are constantly aired in the media during “migration crisis” events, but completely omitted from official statistics by states. Death records are rarely taken in relation to migration status in national-level mortality statistics, which means that deaths related to migration are therefore also “hidden away” in administrative systems. As a result, the work of documenting border deaths falls to a diverse array of actors, including, for example, cemeteries, police departments and non-governmental organisations. This article aims to shed light on the politics and the history of civic record-keeping activities over time through a detailed comparison of two seminal lists of deaths relating to migrants produced by the UNITED network and the International Organisation for Migration’s (IOM) Missing Migrants Project.

UNITED’s list was created in the context of the 1990s anti-racist movement in Germany aiming to draw attention to “death by policy” and state failure. Meanwhile, the IOM’s new project, launched in the aftermath of the Lampedusa shipwreck tragedy in 2013, tends towards the humanitarian and the “naturalisation” of deaths in transit and the repression of the anti-smuggling movement, in order to serve the political and economic interests of different donors and actors within the migration industry. Based on feminist, decolonial and abolitionist theory and drawing on sociological and anthropological knowledge, the article raises questions as to whether the documentation of discriminatory and racist murders simply reproduces the “spectacle of violence” that treats the victims as victims in a human rights NGO context, and whether the data can have a transformative function.

A preprint article (behind a paywall) tries to identify the political mechanisms that cause a disconnect between an increase in deaths at the borders and a political response to the numbers that keep rising. It does this by changing the focus to practices of grassroots and vernacular documentation as produced by families and relatives of those that die on the border; in this way it illustrates an “affective solidarity” built up by feeling and reacting to a multitude of deaths by means of pain, upset and a wish to alter the societal conditions that allow deaths like these to happen. It appears to me that these egalitarian acts document the dead in a way that makes clear to everyone that there is an important class of human life whose mortality society has decided not to take seriously – documenting the dead at borders is a way of letting it be clear whose life matters and whose does not.
